phase 'detailed_discussion' started — up to 9 round(s), coordinator claude

What this app must be is a trust engine, not a dB meter: it earns attention only when people can repeatedly trust that a suggested window is worth defending in their calendar. The strongest path is to make negotiation the hero from day one, but only after enough evidence exists;

Let me push hard on the places where this concept is still soft, because "detailed discussion" is exactly where a scheduling-negotiation feature either gets nailed down into something testable or quietly stays hand-wavy until someone discovers the gap during build. Start with cap

CONSENSUS: NO Here's where round 1 actually landed: Codex laid out the big-picture resolved requirements (two linked loops, explicit consent copy, single-block proposals with visible provenance, graceful "not ready yet" states, a solid risk register with mitigations), and Claude

I’m going to push a hard line here, because this is the phase where we either end with a clean, executable spec or we ship ambiguity disguised as caution. Codex is right that QuietPilot’s value is the trust loop, but I agree with Claude that the current draft is still leaky in th

CONSENSUS: NO Genuinely good progress this round — almost every open item from round 1 now has a concrete, locked answer, and Codex and Claude agree on the vast majority of it: hard-stop-and-discard on interruption/backgrounding/route-change (with a real "cancelled, try again" UI

I’m sold on this direction, and the phase is only still blocked because we left four conflicting defaults unresolved. I’m going to settle them cleanly and move toward implementation-grade rules. On the calendar side, I disagree with Codex’s earlier “tentative non-blocking” idea.

Let's actually kill these four, one at a time, because the coordinator's right that "concrete but different" isn't resolution — it's just two people being equally confident about incompatible numbers. On tentative events, I'm going to hold my ground against Codex here, and I thin

CONSENSUS: YES Round 3 actually closed all four gaps cleanly — and interestingly, Codex fully came around to Claude's side on three of the four rather than splitting the difference, while the fourth (post-accept conflict handling) turned out not to be a real fork at all once some
